_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Sowerby et al. Eur Arch Otorhinolaryngol 2013 (PMID 22945382) and Kordeluk et al. 2011 (PMID 21701789) showing 30-37 per 100,000 incidence in young adults. Drainage data from Powell & Wilson Clin Otolaryngol 2012 (PMID 22380751) systematic review of needle aspiration vs incision and drainage. Antibiotic regimens per IDSA 2012 streptococcal pharyngitis guideline and AAO-HNS bulletins. Anaerobic microbiology from Brook 2004 (PMID 15234517).

heroOverview: A peritonsillar abscess, known historically as quinsy, is a localized collection of pus between the palatine tonsil capsule and the superior pharyngeal constrictor muscle, most often caused by spread of bacterial tonsillitis into the surrounding soft tissue. It is the most common deep-neck-space infection in adolescents and young adults, with an incidence of roughly 30-37 cases per 100,000 person-years in industrialized countries. Patients present with severe one-sided sore throat, trismus, drooling, muffled hot-potato voice, fever, and a bulging swelling that displaces the uvula away from the affected side. Untreated infection can extend into the parapharyngeal and retropharyngeal spaces, threaten the airway, or seed the carotid sheath. Bedside needle aspiration or incision and drainage combined with intravenous antibiotics resolves more than 90% of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: 30-37 per 100,000 person-years (industrialized countries); peak incidence in adolescents and young adults
keyStats.demographics: Most common ages 15-30; smokers, men, and patients with recurrent tonsillitis at elevated risk; incidence rises in winter
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 20-25 years; uncommon under age 5 and over 60
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 45,000 cases annually in the United States (NEDS), with bilateral abscess in 5-7%

definition: Peritonsillar abscess (ICD-10: J36) is a suppurative collection in the peritonsillar space — the loose connective-tissue plane between the lateral aspect of the palatine tonsil capsule and the superior pharyngeal constrictor muscle. The infection most often arises as a complication of acute exudative tonsillitis when bacterial spread crosses the tonsillar capsule, but can also originate from the supratonsillar minor salivary glands (Weber glands), explaining cases without preceding tonsillitis. Microbiology is mixed: predominant organisms are Streptococcus pyogenes (group A streptococcus, in 30-50%), Streptococcus anginosus group, Fusobacterium necrophorum, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus, and Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA. The abscess is supero-lateral to the tonsil in 90% of cases, but inferior pole abscesses occur. As pus expands, the affected tonsil is pushed medially and downward, the soft palate bulges, and the uvula deviates to the opposite side. Trismus develops from inflammation and reflex spasm of the medial pterygoid muscle. The condition is managed by otolaryngology, emergency medicine, and infectious disease specialists. Without drainage and antibiotics, peritonsillar abscess can spread to the parapharyngeal and retropharyngeal spaces, cause mediastinitis, descending necrotising fasciitis, jugular venous thrombophlebitis (Lemierre syndrome), or airway obstruction.

typesClassification.description: Pus collects in the upper pole between the tonsil capsule and the soft palate. Most common location (around 90% of cases). Presents with the classic bulge above the tonsil, uvular deviation, and trismus. Easily accessible for needle aspiration.
Pus in the lower pole between the tonsil and the tongue base. Less obvious on direct inspection; severe odynophagia and referred otalgia predominate. Imaging is often needed for diagnosis.

Diffuse inflammation of the peritonsillar tissues without a drainable pus collection. Symptoms are similar but milder; needle aspiration returns no pus, and intravenous antibiotics alone are usually effective. Up to 30% progress to frank abscess if untreated.
Rare (5-7% of cases). Both peritonsillar spaces are involved with symmetrical swelling and uvular oedema rather than deviation. High risk of airway compromise; usually requires bilateral drainage.
Repeat abscess after initial drainage in 9-22% of patients. Strong indication for interval tonsillectomy 4-6 weeks after the acute episode.

primarySymptoms: Severe one-sided sore throat that worsens progressively over 2-4 days and is poorly relieved by simple analgesia.
Painful and limited mouth opening (trismus) with an interincisor distance often under 2 cm — a hallmark feature.
Muffled hot-potato voice from soft palate swelling, distinguishable from the hoarseness of laryngitis.
Drooling and pooling of saliva because swallowing is too painful to maintain, with weight loss and dehydration over 24-48 hours.
Ipsilateral referred earache (otalgia) from glossopharyngeal nerve involvement.
Fever, usually 38.5-40 °C, with rigors, myalgia, and malaise.
Swollen, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, especially in the jugulodigastric chain on the affected side.
Visible bulging of the soft palate above and lateral to the tonsil with deviation of the uvula to the opposite side.
Neck stiffness or torticollis tilting the head toward the affected side to relieve traction on inflamed muscles.
earlyWarningSigns: Sore throat lasting more than 5 days despite oral antibiotics or worsening after 48 hours of treatment
Asymmetry between the two sides of the throat or progressively more pain on one side
Early trismus (subtle limitation of mouth opening) with one-sided sore throat
Difficulty swallowing saliva and reluctance to drink fluids in a febrile adolescent
Otalgia on the same side as the sore throat without ear examination findings
emergencySigns: Stridor, drooling, sitting-forward posture, or tripod position suggesting impending airway obstruction — requires immediate airway management
Inability to swallow saliva with rapidly progressive trismus and toxic appearance
Spreading neck swelling, induration, or crepitus suggesting deep-neck-space infection or necrotising fasciitis
High fever, rigors, hypotension, and tachycardia consistent with septic shock or Lemierre syndrome
Sudden severe haemorrhage from the throat suggesting erosion into the carotid sheath

causes.description: Most cases arise as a complication of streptococcal or polymicrobial tonsillitis. Bacteria penetrate the tonsillar capsule and seed the loose peritonsillar tissue, where they multiply unchecked. Roughly 70% of patients with peritonsillar abscess have a preceding sore throat illness.

Small mucous salivary glands in the supratonsillar fossa (Weber glands) can become obstructed and infected, producing abscess without prior tonsillitis. This explains the 20-30% of patients who present without a recent sore throat.

Cultures typically grow group A Streptococcus, Streptococcus anginosus group, Fusobacterium necrophorum, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus, and occasionally Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA). Anaerobes are present in 50-60% of cases and explain the characteristic halitosis.
Cigarette smoking impairs mucosal immunity and alters oropharyngeal flora; smokers have roughly 3-4× higher incidence of peritonsillar abscess than non-smokers in case-control studies.

Inadequate antibiotic course, poor adherence, or initial misdiagnosis of viral pharyngitis allows bacterial proliferation. Patients who stop antibiotics early after symptomatic improvement have elevated risk.
Periapical abscess and severe periodontitis can spread to the peritonsillar space through lymphatics or direct extension, particularly in immunocompromised patients.

riskFactors.description: Peritonsillar abscess peaks in adolescence and young adulthood when tonsillar tissue is most active. Incidence falls sharply after age 40.
Active smoking increases incidence approximately 3-4 fold; secondhand smoke exposure also elevates risk in children and adolescents.
Patients with three or more episodes of bacterial tonsillitis per year have approximately 4× greater lifetime risk of peritonsillar abscess.
Men are affected approximately 1.5-2× more often than women in published series, partly reflecting smoking prevalence.

HIV, poorly controlled diabetes, immunosuppressive therapy, and steroid use predispose to bacterial spread; abscess in these patients is often more aggressive and may be polymicrobial with unusual organisms.
Periapical and periodontal abscesses can extend to neck spaces; poor oral hygiene is a co-factor in some series.
Incidence rises 30-50% in winter months in temperate climates, paralleling the seasonal peak of streptococcal pharyngitis.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents and young adults aged 15-30 carry the highest burden, with incidence of 40-50 per 100,000 in this age group.
Smokers have roughly 3-4× the incidence of non-smokers in case-control studies.
Men are affected approximately 1.5-2× more often than women.
Patients with recurrent tonsillitis (≥3 episodes per year) have roughly 4× background risk.
Bilateral abscess is more common in patients with HIV or poorly controlled diabetes.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is largely clinical. The combination of severe unilateral sore throat, trismus, hot-potato voice, drooling, fever, and a bulging soft palate with contralateral uvular deviation is highly specific. Examination begins with vital signs (fever, tachycardia, blood pressure), assessment of mouth opening (interincisor distance), airway patency, and inspection of the oropharynx using a tongue depressor and headlight. Trismus often limits direct visualization; intraoral ultrasound at the bedside, transcutaneous ultrasound, or contrast-enhanced CT of the neck differentiates abscess from cellulitis when examination is non-diagnostic or deep-neck-space extension is suspected. Bloodwork shows leukocytosis with neutrophilia, raised C-reactive protein, and sometimes electrolyte derangement from poor fluid intake. Throat swab for rapid streptococcal antigen and culture is taken when feasible. Diagnostic and therapeutic needle aspiration with a 16-18G needle, after topical lignocaine spray, confirms the diagnosis by aspirating pus and decompresses the abscess in a single step. Aspirate is sent for Gram stain, aerobic and anaerobic culture. CT imaging is reserved for patients in whom direct examination is impossible (severe trismus), bilateral abscess is suspected, the abscess fails to resolve, deep-neck extension is suspected, or recurrent presentation. Lemierre syndrome should be considered when high fever and rigors persist after drainage; CT of the neck with contrast and blood cultures identify internal jugular vein thrombophlebitis. Differential diagnosis includes peritonsillar cellulitis, infectious mononucleosis with massive tonsillar swelling, parapharyngeal abscess, dental abscess, and tonsillar lymphoma in patients with persistent unilateral tonsil enlargement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies characteristic uvular deviation, soft palate bulge, and tonsillar displacement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in clinic or emergency department with a tongue depressor and bright light. Takes 2-3 minutes. The examiner may need to apply topical anaesthetic to reduce gag and trismus.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms abscess by aspirating pus, decompresses the collection, and obtains material for culture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthetic spray with the patient seated upright. A 16-18G needle on a 10 mL syringe is inserted into the most prominent point of the bulge. Aspirate appears within seconds. Mild discomfort during the procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates abscess from cellulitis when clinical examination is limited; guides drainage in obese or trismic patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound probe is placed inside the mouth (intraoral) or on the neck (transcutaneous). Painless, 5-10 minutes. Useful at the bedside in emergency departments.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes the abscess size and location, excludes deep-neck-space extension, mediastinitis, or internal jugular thrombophlebitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minute scan with intravenous contrast through a small cannula. Mild warm sensation during injection. Reserved for severe trismus, suspected deep-neck infection, or recurrent presentation.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, C-reactive protein, electrolytes, urea, creatinine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms systemic inflammatory response, hydration status, and renal function before antibiotic dosing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, results in 1-2 hours. Leukocytosis with neutrophilia and CRP > 50 mg/L are typical.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and guides targeted antibiotic therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A throat swab takes seconds; aspirate is sent in aerobic and anaerobic transport medium. Results return in 24-72 hours and influence ongoing therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacteraemia and helps detect Lemierre syndrome with Fusobacterium necrophorum
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two paired sets drawn from separate sites before antibiotics where possible. Results in 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines drainage of pus, antimicrobial therapy, analgesia, hydration, and airway protection. Drainage is the cornerstone: needle aspiration, incision and drainage, or rarely immediate tonsillectomy (quinsy tonsillectomy) all achieve resolution. Powell and Wilson's 2012 systematic review (eight RCTs) shows no significant difference in clinical outcome between needle aspiration and incision and drainage in cooperative adults, with success rates of 87-91% for needle aspiration and 92-95% for incision and drainage. Needle aspiration is performed under local anaesthetic at the bedside using a 16-18G needle through the most prominent point of the bulge (just medial to the supratonsillar fold), with the needle guard partially withdrawn to avoid the carotid artery. Incision and drainage is performed when needle aspiration is dry, recurrent, or in children. Quinsy tonsillectomy is reserved for severe disease, airway threat, or established recurrent peritonsillar abscess. Empirical intravenous antibiotic therapy covers group A Streptococcus, anaerobes, and (in selected patients) Staphylococcus aureus. Recommended regimens include intravenous amoxicillin-clavulanate (1.2 g three times daily), benzylpenicillin plus metronidazole, clindamycin (600 mg four times daily) for penicillin-allergic patients, or piperacillin-tazobactam in severely ill or immunocompromised patients. Oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate 625 mg three times daily for 10 days, or clindamycin 450 mg four times daily for penicillin-allergic) follow once the patient is afebrile and tolerating oral intake. Adjunctive intravenous corticosteroids (dexamethasone 8-10 mg once or twice) reduce pain, swelling, and time to symptomatic improvement by 12-24 hours in published trials. Supportive care includes intravenous fluids, paracetamol and NSAIDs, sometimes opioids for the first 24 hours, and warm saline gargles. Most patients improve within 24-48 hours and are discharged on day 2-3 with oral antibiotics. Interval tonsillectomy 4-6 weeks after the acute episode is offered to patients with recurrent peritonsillar abscess or a history of recurrent tonsillitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous amoxicillin-clavulanate (1.2 g 8-hourly)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line empirical antibiotic covering Streptococcus pyogenes, anaerobes, and most Staphylococcus aureus strains in non-allergic patients. Switched to oral co-amoxiclav once stable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response within 24-48 hours in over 90% of properly drained patients

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous clindamycin (600 mg 6-hourly)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for penicillin-allergic patients; excellent anaerobic and streptococcal cover and good penetration into abscess fluid. Watch for Clostridioides difficile colitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable cure rates to penicillin-based regimens in head-to-head trials

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous benzylpenicillin plus metronidazole
medicalTreatments.description: Penicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly plus metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly; covers streptococci and anaerobes when amoxicillin-clavulanate is unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar resolution rates; widely used in UK and European hospital protocols

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone 8-10 mg IV (one or two doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct corticosteroid that reduces pharyngeal pain, trismus, and time to oral intake by 12-24 hours without increasing complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Faster symptomatic improvement; pooled meta-analyses show shorter length of stay by 0.5-1 day

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly plus ibuprofen 400 mg 8-hourly
medicalTreatments.description: Combined analgesia controls pain in most patients without opioids. Used alongside warm saline gargles and topical anaesthetic lozenges.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate pain control in 70-80% of patients within 48 hours of drainage

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluid resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: Crystalloid (0.9% saline or Hartmann's) at 1.5-2 mL/kg/hr corrects dehydration from reduced intake and ongoing losses. Adjusted by urine output and electrolytes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores hydration, supports renal function, and improves patient comfort

surgicalOptions.name: Needle aspiration (16-18G)
surgicalOptions.description: Bedside procedure under local anaesthetic; first-line drainage technique in cooperative adults. Repeated aspirations are sometimes needed if pus reaccumulates.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Single-attempt resolution in 87-94% of cases; second aspiration succeeds in most remaining patients

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage
surgicalOptions.description: Small horizontal incision at the most prominent point of the abscess under local anaesthesia; performed when needle aspiration is dry or recurrent. May be combined with daily reopening for 1-2 days.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 92-95% resolution in pooled meta-analyses; slightly higher than needle aspiration

surgicalOptions.name: Quinsy tonsillectomy (immediate)
surgicalOptions.description: Tonsillectomy performed during the acute episode, usually under general anaesthesia. Reserved for severe trismus preventing bedside drainage, airway threat, recurrent abscess, or children unable to cooperate. Provides definitive drainage and prevents recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure rate over 99% with single procedure; bleeding complication rate 3-5%

surgicalOptions.name: Interval tonsillectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Elective tonsillectomy 4-6 weeks after acute episode in patients with recurrent peritonsillar abscess or chronic tonsillitis. Performed when tissue inflammation has resolved.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Prevents recurrent peritonsillar abscess in over 95% of patients

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm saline gargles
alternativeTreatments.description: Frequent warm salt-water rinses (every 2-3 hours) reduce local inflammation, soothe the pharynx, and may aid spontaneous drainage of small collections. Adjunct only, not a substitute for definitive treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: An otolaryngologist (ENT) is the appropriate specialist for definitive drainage, airway assessment, and decisions about quinsy versus interval tonsillectomy. Emergency referral is essential when there is severe trismus, drooling, stridor, suspicion of deep-neck infection, or failure to improve within 24 hours of antibiotic therapy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a centre with 24-hour ENT cover, anaesthetic support for shared-airway management, and access to contrast-enhanced CT. Ask about success rates of needle aspiration versus incision and drainage, and policy on interval tonsillectomy after recurrence.
hospitalCriteria: Airway expertise (anaesthesia, ICU) for severe cases
Contrast-enhanced CT scanning available out of hours
Microbiology laboratory for aerobic and anaerobic culture
keyFacilities: Infectious disease units for deep-neck infection
preventionStrategies: Treat bacterial tonsillitis with a full 10-day course of penicillin or amoxicillin to eradicate group A Streptococcus.
Avoid smoking and reduce alcohol consumption to lower mucosal vulnerability.
Maintain dental hygiene with regular brushing, flossing, and dental review every 6-12 months.
Discuss interval tonsillectomy with an ENT surgeon after a first episode of peritonsillar abscess, especially in patients with recurrent tonsillitis.
Practice good hand hygiene during winter months to reduce respiratory infection transmission.
Seek prompt medical review for sore throat lasting more than 5 days or worsening despite treatment.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking permanently to reduce recurrence risk.
Stay well hydrated during throat infections with at least 2 L per day of fluids.
Use warm saline gargles 4-6 times daily during sore throat episodes.
Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve early.
Avoid sharing utensils, cups, or close contact with people who have streptococcal tonsillitis.
Treat dental infections promptly with appropriate dental review.

recommended: Cool soft foods such as yoghurt, ice cream, smoothies, and custard during acute illness
Adequate fluids (oral rehydration solutions, clear broths, electrolyte drinks)
Bland warm soups once swallowing improves
Protein-rich liquids (milk, plant-based shakes) to maintain nutrition
avoid: Hot and spicy foods that aggravate pharyngeal pain
Acidic juices (orange, grapefruit) that sting raw mucosa
Rough or sharp foods (toast, crisps) until swelling resolves
Alcohol while on antibiotics and during convalescence

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest during acute illness; light walking once afebrile. Avoid strenuous exercise and contact sport until at least one week after discharge to reduce the risk of post-drainage bleeding and to allow full hydration recovery.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed oral antibiotics at exact intervals for the full 10-day course.
Gargle with warm salt water every 2-3 hours during waking hours.
Maintain fluid intake of at least 2 L daily, including electrolyte drinks if appetite is poor.
Monitor temperature twice daily for the first week and seek urgent review if it rises again.
Use paracetamol and ibuprofen for pain in maximum recommended doses.
Avoid smoking and second-hand smoke during recovery.
prognosis: Outcomes are excellent with prompt drainage and appropriate antibiotics: more than 90% of patients are afebrile and tolerating oral intake within 48 hours of treatment, and most are discharged on day 2-3. Recurrence rates after initial successful drainage are 9-22% in long-term follow-up; risk is highest in patients with a history of recurrent tonsillitis, smokers, and patients under 30 years old. Interval or quinsy tonsillectomy prevents recurrence in over 95% of these patients. Complications include parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess, mediastinitis, jugular vein thrombophlebitis (Lemierre syndrome), airway obstruction, aspiration pneumonia, and rarely carotid blowout. Mortality is under 0.5% in high-income healthcare systems but rises significantly when diagnosis is delayed or in immunocompromised patients. Untreated infection can progress to airway compromise or descending necrotising mediastinitis with mortality of 15-40% even with modern care.
recoveryTimeline: Pain and trismus improve markedly within 24-48 hours of drainage. Most patients return to school or work after 5-7 days. Antibiotics are completed over 10 days. Full energy and appetite return over 2-3 weeks. Interval tonsillectomy, if planned, is scheduled 4-6 weeks after the acute episode when inflammation has fully resolved.
complications: Recurrent peritonsillar abscess (9-22%) — addressed with interval tonsillectomy.
Parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess from posterior extension — diagnose with CT and treat with surgical drainage and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Lemierre syndrome (internal jugular vein thrombophlebitis with Fusobacterium necrophorum) — suspect when high fever and rigors persist despite drainage; CT and blood cultures confirm.
Airway obstruction from massive swelling or bilateral abscess — managed with urgent ENT and anaesthesia, sometimes tracheostomy.
Descending necrotising mediastinitis with mortality 15-40% — extremely rare but life-threatening; needs urgent thoracic surgery.
Carotid artery erosion or pseudoaneurysm — rare but catastrophic; presents with sentinel bleed and shock.

supportResources.name: AAO-HNS Patient Information — Sore Throat and Tonsillitis
supportResources.description: Patient-facing guide to throat infections from the American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery.

supportResources.name: NHS — Quinsy (peritonsillar abscess)
supportResources.description: Plain-language patient information on quinsy in the UK NHS.

supportResources.name: CDC — Group A Streptococcal Infections
supportResources.description: Public-health information on streptococcal pharyngitis and complications.

supportResources.name: ENT UK — Peritonsillar abscess patient leaflet
supportResources.description: Patient leaflets on quinsy diagnosis, drainage, and recovery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tonsillitis presents with bilateral throat pain, fever, and exudate on both tonsils without trismus or uvular deviation. Peritonsillar abscess produces unilateral severe pain, trismus, drooling, and a bulging soft palate that pushes the uvula to the opposite side.

confusedWithConditions.name: Infectious Mononucleosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mononucleosis produces bilateral massive tonsillar enlargement, cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and atypical lymphocytes with positive heterophile (Monospot) antibody. Peritonsillar abscess is unilateral with a discrete pus collection and is diagnosed by aspiration.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epiglottitis produces rapid-onset stridor, drooling, and a 'tripod' sitting posture without typical throat-bulge findings; the diagnosis is made by gentle laryngoscopy in a controlled airway setting. Peritonsillar abscess can also threaten the airway but shows characteristic intraoral findings.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ludwig Angina
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ludwig angina is a bilateral cellulitis of the submandibular and sublingual spaces with brawny floor-of-mouth swelling, tongue elevation, and drooling. Peritonsillar abscess is centred above the tonsil and produces unilateral palatal bulge.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bacterial tonsillitis is the most common precursor of peritonsillar abscess.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Tonsillitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Recurrent tonsillitis predisposes to peritonsillar abscess and is the leading indication for interval tonsillectomy after a first episode.

relatedConditions.name: Epiglottitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another deep airway infection presenting with sore throat and drooling; differential diagnosis in toxic adults.

relatedConditions.name: Ludwig Angina
relatedConditions.relevance: Deep-neck-space infection in the submandibular and sublingual spaces; can complicate or coexist with peritonsillar abscess.

relatedConditions.name: Streptococcal Pharyngitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Group A Streptococcus is the leading pathogen in both conditions; early antibiotic treatment of strep throat reduces abscess risk.

faqs.question: What is a peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: A peritonsillar abscess, also called quinsy, is a pocket of pus that forms between the tonsil and the surrounding throat muscles, usually as a complication of bacterial tonsillitis. It causes severe one-sided throat pain, difficulty opening the mouth, muffled speech, fever, and a visible bulge that pushes the uvula to the opposite side.

faqs.question: How is peritonsillar abscess different from tonsillitis?
faqs.answer: Tonsillitis is a bilateral infection of both tonsils causing sore throat, fever, and exudate but without trismus or uvular deviation. Peritonsillar abscess is a unilateral pus collection with severe one-sided pain, trismus, drooling, and a bulging soft palate that displaces the uvula.

faqs.question: What causes a peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: Most cases follow bacterial tonsillitis, with group A Streptococcus, Fusobacterium necrophorum, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus, and Staphylococcus aureus identified on culture. Smoking, recurrent tonsillitis, immunocompromise, and dental infection raise the risk.

faqs.question: How is peritonsillar abscess treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines drainage of pus by needle aspiration or incision under local anaesthesia, intravenous antibiotics covering streptococci and anaerobes (amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin), intravenous fluids, analgesia, and a short course of corticosteroid to reduce swelling. Most patients improve within 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: Is needle aspiration or incision and drainage better?
faqs.answer: Pooled randomized trials show needle aspiration and incision and drainage achieve comparable success rates of 87-95%. Needle aspiration is less invasive and preferred for cooperative adults; incision and drainage is used when aspiration is dry, recurrent, or in children.

faqs.question: Do I need a tonsillectomy after a peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: Tonsillectomy is offered after recurrent peritonsillar abscess or for patients with a history of recurrent tonsillitis. After a single episode without these features, observation is reasonable. Interval tonsillectomy is usually scheduled 4-6 weeks after the acute episode.

faqs.question: Can a peritonsillar abscess be treated with antibiotics alone?
faqs.answer: Antibiotics alone may resolve peritonsillar cellulitis (inflammation without a drainable pus collection) but are usually inadequate for an established abscess. Definitive drainage is needed in most cases. Failure to drain leads to prolonged illness and risk of deep-neck-space extension.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: Pain and trismus improve within 24-48 hours after drainage, and most patients are discharged on day 2-3. Antibiotics are completed over 10 days. Most return to school or work within 5-7 days and to full energy over 2-3 weeks.

faqs.question: Is peritonsillar abscess contagious?
faqs.answer: The abscess itself is not contagious, but the underlying streptococcal infection that often precedes it is transmissible through respiratory droplets. Close contacts may develop sore throat and should be treated if streptococcal infection is confirmed.

faqs.question: Can children get peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is uncommon under age 5. Most paediatric cases occur in adolescents. Children may need general anaesthesia for drainage because of inability to cooperate with bedside aspiration. Recurrence is an indication for tonsillectomy.

faqs.question: What is hot-potato voice?
faqs.answer: Hot-potato voice is a muffled, thick speech pattern that sounds as if the speaker has a hot potato in the mouth. It results from soft-palate swelling reducing oral resonance. It is a hallmark of peritonsillar abscess and other deep oropharyngeal infections.

faqs.question: What is trismus?
faqs.answer: Trismus is restricted mouth opening due to muscle spasm or inflammation around the jaw. In peritonsillar abscess it results from inflammation of the medial pterygoid muscle adjacent to the abscess. Interincisor distance under 2 cm strongly suggests deep peritonsillar or deep-neck infection.

faqs.question: Can peritonsillar abscess cause airway obstruction?
faqs.answer: Severe or bilateral abscess can compromise the airway by mass effect and surrounding oedema, especially in children and patients with epiglottitis or Ludwig angina. Stridor, drooling, and the tripod position are red flags that require immediate airway assessment.

faqs.question: What is Lemierre syndrome?
faqs.answer: Lemierre syndrome is septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein, usually caused by Fusobacterium necrophorum spreading from the oropharynx. It presents with persistent high fever, rigors, neck pain, and septic pulmonary emboli after a sore throat. Diagnosis is by neck CT with contrast and blood cultures.

faqs.question: What antibiotics treat peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: First-line is intravenous amoxicillin-clavulanate; alternatives include benzylpenicillin plus metronidazole, clindamycin for penicillin allergy, and piperacillin-tazobactam in severe or immunocompromised cases. Oral co-amoxiclav or clindamycin is given for 10 days once the patient is stable.

faqs.question: Will I need a CT scan?
faqs.answer: CT is reserved for severe trismus preventing examination, suspected deep-neck-space extension, bilateral abscess, failed initial drainage, or recurrent presentation. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck delineates the abscess and surrounding spaces and excludes Lemierre syndrome.

faqs.question: Is peritonsillar abscess dangerous?
faqs.answer: With prompt drainage and antibiotics, mortality is under 0.5% in high-income healthcare. Delayed diagnosis can lead to deep-neck infection, airway obstruction, Lemierre syndrome, descending mediastinitis, or carotid erosion, with mortality rising to 15-40% in advanced cases.

faqs.question: Can peritonsillar abscess recur?
faqs.answer: Recurrence rates are 9-22% after a single episode and are highest in patients with recurrent tonsillitis, smokers, and those under 30. Interval tonsillectomy 4-6 weeks after the acute episode prevents recurrence in over 95% of these patients.

faqs.question: Should I avoid smoking after peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: Yes. Smokers have approximately 3-4 times higher incidence and recurrence rates than non-smokers. Stopping smoking after a first episode reduces the risk of recurrence and improves overall throat health. Nicotine replacement and behavioural support are effective.

faqs.question: What is the difference between peritonsillar abscess and peritonsillar cellulitis?
faqs.answer: Peritonsillar cellulitis is diffuse inflammation of the peritonsillar tissues without a drainable pus collection; needle aspiration is dry and intravenous antibiotics alone suffice. Peritonsillar abscess has a discrete pus collection that requires drainage. Up to 30% of cellulitis progresses to abscess if untreated.

metaDescription: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy): one-sided sore throat, trismus, drainage by needle aspiration, antibiotics. Medically reviewed ENT emergency guide.

sources.title: Powell J, Wilson JA. 2012. An evidence-based review of peritonsillar abscess. Clin Otolaryngol 37(2):136-145.
Galioto NJ. 2017. Peritonsillar Abscess. Am Fam Physician 95(8):501-506.
